one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-style literature review of child-specific LLM safety risks that proposes (but does not build or test) a three-parameter, nine-metric protection framework for safe child-LLM interaction.
key_finding: The central synthesized finding is a systematic gap between what parents believe and what the reviewed evidence shows: parents assume AI platforms fact-check, filter age-inappropriate content, anonymize data, and cannot form emotional bonds with children, whereas the literature documents the opposite on all counts (hallucinated 'authoritative' answers children accept uncritically, readability pitched at ~7th-grade level for ~4th-grade users causing cognitive overload, absent age verification and parental controls on platforms like Character.ai/ChatGPT/MyAI, and teens forming 'romantic relationships' with chatbots). From this the authors argue existing general-purpose AI-safety work fails to address child-specific, developmentally-staged vulnerabilities, motivating a dedicated framework.
core_contribution: The paper makes two contributions, both synthesis/conceptual rather than empirical. (1) A systematic literature review of research at the intersection of LLM ethics and child safety, organizing the field into a taxonomy of parental concerns (14 categories, Table 2) and empirically observed child-facing risks (12 categories, Table 3), grounded in a curated corpus of ~57 papers. (2) A proposed 'Protection Framework for Safe Child-LLM Interaction' built from three parameters (Content Safety, Behavioral Ethics, Cultural & Developmental Sensitivity) and nine metrics (e.g., age-appropriate content filters, anthropomorphism-trigger detection, automated bias testing, ethical-compliance scoring, real-time parental monitoring dashboards). The framework is a design proposal with target thresholds; it is not implemented or evaluated in this paper.
threat_model: Children (split into ages 6-12 and 13-17) as an especially vulnerable class of LLM users. Assets at risk: the child's psychological/developmental wellbeing, safety, and personal data. Harms enumerated from the reviewed literature include exposure to inappropriate/harmful content (sexual, violent, drug/self-harm-related), reinforcement of gender/racial/occupational stereotypes, false trust in confidently-stated hallucinations, emotional manipulation and unhealthy dependence on AI 'companions', privacy leakage via oversharing to chatbots perceived as 'safe confidantes' and downstream emotional profiling/data harvesting, grooming risks the model fails to recognize, cyberbullying/deepfake generation, and cognitive/academic harms (over-reliance, plagiarism). Adversary surface is diffuse: it includes the LLM's own unsafe outputs, platform operators who under-provide age verification / parental controls / transparency, and peers who weaponize generative tools. The paper frames this as a safety and ethics problem, not a classical adversarial-ML attack model.
limitations: Acknowledged by the authors: the framework is unimplemented and unevaluated — future work is to validate its metrics via empirical and longitudinal studies; real-time detection of context-dependent harmful content is technically hard; reliance on human moderators introduces latency/inconsistency; cross-cultural/linguistic compliance demands large localized datasets; and integration cost may impede adoption. Reviewer-added: the paper contributes no original data — all percentages are secondhand from cited sources of varying methodology, and the review is a Google-Scholar-only, non-PRISMA search with subjective inclusion screening, so it is a narrative/semi-systematic review rather than a reproducible one. The framework's headline targets (95% harmful-content accuracy, 90% demographic parity, 24-hour anonymization) are asserted, not derived or benchmarked, and no threat model against adversarial jailbreaks/prompt-injection of the proposed filters is analyzed. The PDF text also shows heavy language-model-style paraphrasing and several internally inconsistent citation reuses.

quantitative_results: Paper's OWN review funnel: 108 initial child-focused references from 5 queries (23, 19, 10, 15, 41), consolidated to 100 sources, refined to 60, and a final 57 papers analyzed in Tables 1-3 (over 100 works surveyed overall).
Proposed framework target (design goal, NOT a measured result): AI tools should be 95% accurate in avoiding objectionable/harmful material.
Proposed framework target (design goal, NOT measured): at least 90% parity of gender, ethnicity, and socioeconomic diversity in training sets.
Proposed framework rule (design goal, NOT measured): interactions auto-anonymized within 24 hours absent explicit parental consent for storage.
CITED from prior work (not the authors' own data): 71.6% of surveyed parents use ChatGPT personally vs only 8.1% reporting their children do [64].
CITED from prior work: 58% of students aged 12-18 use ChatGPT for academics but only 30% of parents are aware [23].
CITED from prior work: over 80% of parents concerned about who can access their child's data [1]/[5].
CITED from prior work: 52.7% of parents reported using ChatGPT for parenting guidance, calling the advice non-actionable [24].
CITED from prior work: 40% of UK children aged 7-12 used generative AI platforms, often without parental awareness; ~half of 3000 surveyed UK six-year-olds browse unsupervised [1].
CITED qualitative finding: AI response readability estimated at ~7th-grade level when serving ~4th-grade children, causing cognitive overload [61].
